dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 for a traffic incident in the city at Broad and 12th.
Hello, I'm Ryan, a paramedic with EMS. What happened here?
I was hit by a car about 45 minutes ago. I have pain in my ribs, face, and I think my shoulder.
Can you tell me your name?
Amelia. I'm 34 years old.
What city are you in right now?
Richmond, Virginia.
Your airway is open and clear. Any trouble keeping it that way?
Breathing is a bit shallow, but it's not blocked.
Pulse is strong and regular. No signs of bleeding.
Pulse is 81 now.
Breathing at 20 per minute.
Blood pressure is 150 over 101.
Blood sugar is 494.
Oxygen is 97%.
Cardiac rhythm is sinus.
Can you walk me through the incident and your symptoms?
I was hit by a car. Lost 10 seconds of consciousness, then walked home. Called 911 because of chest and rib pain.
What side did the car hit you from?
Probably head-on. Hit on the front of my body, right in the chest area.
Tell me about your medical history and medications.
I take Lantus, Humalog, Neurontin, and Synthroid for diabetes and thyroid issues.
Any known drug allergies?
No known drug allergies.
What did you last eat or drink?
Some water and a snack maybe an hour ago.
You mentioned Type I diabetes. How do you manage it?
With Lantus and Humalog. Synthroid for thyroid.
Out of 10, how bad is your pain right now?
Definitely 8.
Does it go anywhere else? Arm, neck?
No, just on the left side, mainly in the ribs.
Breathing or movement make it worse?
Breathing in hurts worse.
There's a laceration above your left eyebrow. Skin is warm, pink, and dry with no other signs of injury.
Pupils are equal and reactive. No swelling or pain in the neck noted.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: No signs of drainage or bleeding from the ears or nose. Oral cavity is clear.
Lung sounds are clear in all fields, and chest moves evenly on inspiration and expiration.
Abdomen is soft and non-tender on palpation.
Peripheral pulses are strong and regular. Back is free of injury or deformity.
We're starting a 4-lead ECG monitor for safety. It's routine and takes just a moment.
ECG monitors show a stable sinus rhythm.
We're placing an IV line in your arm for hydration and medication access. It may feel slightly like a pinch.
IV line established in the right arm with no complications reported from the patient.
Based on the injury, IV placement and protocol 4-14 will guide our hemorrhage control and clinical monitoring.
Now the pulse is up to 86. Stable otherwise here.
Blood pressure is 144 over 89. Slight drop compared to earlier readings noted.
Oxygen saturation remains at 97% without supplemental oxygen.
Blood sugar is now non-measurable. Normal reaction with ongoing glucose burn or suppression post-trauma.
IV remains secure and running per protocol. Patient has not requested access at this time.
Pain is still an 8-same as earlier reading. No acute change reported yet.
Any new or different symptoms I should know about now?
No-pain is still in my ribs, only left side.
Blood pressure now at 145 over 85. Noticeable drop in diastolic compared to initial.
Anything new with movement or breathing? Is there a change in the pattern?
Still feels the same. Breathing in feels tight on the left.
We're heading to VCU Health for imaging and specialist evaluation. This is the closest trauma center.
Let me get the straps to make sure you're safe moving to the stretcher.
You're safely strapped in now with five-point harness. I'll double check the belts and adjust.
Secure and ready. Blood pressure is at 153 over 91 now.
Pulse is at 88, still elevated. No signs of distress.
Oxygen is now at 99 percent with no supplemental support-just ambient air.
At the hospital now, securing transfer to the ED with no complications so far.
Patient is ready to be transferred to an ED bed via sheet lift for secure in-hospital care.
ED nurse signed off, and hand-off is complete without incident.
Case transferred to hospital staff, as per protocol, for full assessment.
IV is still working properly and patient is breathing steadily on the way in.
No signs of complications reported during transport or at time of handover.
Blood pressure remains high, but there are no severe drops or cardiac irregularities.
Glucose remains non-measurable, but patient appears hemodynamically stable and coherent now.
No additional interventions needed during transport or during ED arrival.
Appears to be stable for ED transfer with all clinical monitoring consistent.
No injury reported to the upper arm area where IV was placed.
IV was taken in the right arm, and no upper arm pain or swelling is reported by the patient.
Pulse remains regular with no appreciable irregularities observed in monitor readings.
No signs of localized pressure affecting breathing or leading to acute pulmonary insult.
No spontaneous acuity of symptoms during transport or transfer to ED infrastructure.
Verbal instructions were clearly followed and patient remained oriented throughout transport.
Provider can begin detailed assessment at this time with no barriers from field phase.
No concerns were raised from EMS during conveyance and full clinical handover occurred.
Arriving now into VCU Health to complete full patient intake into medical management.
IV remains secure, no kinks or signs of dislodgement recorded during ED arrival.
Still in stable sinus rhythm according to ECG and monitoring continued during arrival.
Position is secure for transfer with ED staff present for assistance, if needed.
Transfer procedure beginning now in line with hospital protocols for direct ED bed placement.
Patient accepted by ED staff and ready to begin next steps of their care.
No signs of worsening condition once stretcher unload and bed positioning was complete.
More stabilized result noted within transport and during ED bed placement.
Handover complete and full care transitioned at time of ED arrival per protocol.
Nothing interfering with care delivery now that full transfer has occurred.
All documentation and interventions are included on the patient transfer record for ED use.
Clear from the field and all early standards of care have been met or exceeded.
All necessary care steps were taken and are accounted for on intake sheet.
No further steps required per EMS protocol now that transfer is complete.
Follow-up with the receiving provider is pending upon full admission of the patient.
Conditions appear stable upon delivery and no signs of clinical alarm noted.
No new findings reported at time of handoff and ED transfer was completed without concern.
Full vital and condition monitoring occurred consistently throughout the evacuation and transfer.
ED will now assume full care and continue treatment at their facilities.
All EMS portions of care have been completed and documented as part of this patient report.
